worth_it_explanation: Curation lets a third party overlay a publisher's inventory with audience data and package it into a deal that buyers target directly, which can bring demand that would not have reached the inventory through the open exchange alone. A curation setup that maps the right inventory to the right packages, and that gets pruned as inventory and audiences change, keeps that demand flowing. The check is a review of the curated deals and packages a publisher already has in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete mapping or maintenance decision the publisher can act on.
intro: Microsoft Curate is the curation product that lets curators package a publisher's inventory together with their own audience data or supply rules and sell it to buyers as a deal. On the sell side, a publisher sees curated transactions compete in the final auction alongside open-exchange and direct-deal bids, so the value of curation depends on which inventory is exposed to it and how cleanly the packages map to that inventory. This subcategory covers two questions. The first is whether the curated deals and packages are set up against the inventory the publisher actually means to expose. The second is whether those packages are maintained over time rather than left to drift as inventory and audience data change.

audit_checks.title: Curated deals are not mapped to the inventory the publisher means to expose
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Curation overlays a third party's data onto a publisher's inventory and exposes it to buyers as a deal. If the inventory mapped into curated deals is broader or narrower than the publisher intends, premium placements can end up curated at terms the publisher did not mean to offer, or inventory the publisher wanted curated can sit outside every package and miss the added demand. Either way the mapping diverges from intent on a slice of inventory.

educate.base:
Microsoft Curate lets a curator, such as a retailer, a data company, or a trading desk, package a publisher's inventory together with their own audience data or supply rules and offer it to buyers as a curated deal. On the sell side the publisher does not lose control of the auction. Microsoft's own documentation is explicit that curated transactions are not guaranteed deals, do not override seller account settings such as floor prices or ad quality controls, and have to win the final auction net of fees like any other bid. What curation does change is which buyers see the inventory and on what terms, so the inventory a publisher exposes to curation is a deliberate choice rather than a default.

The audit question is whether the curated deals in place map to the inventory the publisher actually means to expose. A mapping that is too broad pulls premium placements into a curated package at terms set for general inventory, so a homepage or sponsorship-adjacent unit clears through curation when the publisher meant to hold it for direct sale. A mapping that is too narrow leaves inventory the publisher wanted curated outside every package, so it never gets the extra demand the curator's data was meant to bring. Both are the same defect seen from opposite ends, which is a gap between the inventory in the curated deals and the inventory the publisher intended to curate.

The mapping also runs through which curators a publisher works with. Microsoft Monetize lets a seller block transactions with specific members through the Partner Center screen, and curators are identified with a '[CURATOR]' tag at the end of their member name. A publisher who has not reviewed which curators can package their inventory is mapping by omission since every unblocked curator can overlay the inventory rather than only the ones the publisher chose.

A few signs point to a curation mapping that has drifted from intent:
  • Premium or direct-sold placements appear inside curated deals that were set up for general inventory
  • Inventory the publisher wanted to expose to curation sits outside every curated package
  • The publisher cannot name which curators are enabled or which inventory each curated deal covers
You may want to confirm that the inventory inside each curated deal matches what the publisher meant to expose, and that the enabled curators are the ones the publisher chose. One approach is to list the active curated deals against the inventory they cover and reconcile that list with the publisher's stated intent for which inventory should be curated and by whom.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, homepage and section-front placements often carry direct-sold value the publisher protects, while article-body and lower-page units are better candidates for curation demand. You may want to confirm the premium placements are not swept into a general curated package, and that the curated deals cover the lower-priority inventory where the curator's audience data adds the most.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience close to a purchase, which is exactly the audience a retail or shopping-data curator wants to package. You may want to confirm the high-intent commercial inventory is mapped into the curated deals where that demand concentrates, rather than leaving it in the open exchange while only the editorial pages are curated.

investigate.intro: Review the active curated deals against the inventory each covers and the curators that are enabled, then reconcile that against the publisher's stated intent for which inventory should be curated and by whom.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize UI
steps: Open the curated deals view and list the active curated deals along with the inventory each one targets.
For each deal, note which ad units, placements, or inventory groups it covers and whether any premium or direct-sold inventory falls inside it.
Open the Partner Center screen and list the members enabled to transact, identifying curators by the '[CURATOR]' tag at the end of the member name.
Ask the publisher which inventory they intended to expose to curation and which curators they meant to work with, then record where the active setup diverges from that intent.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize Network Analytics report
steps: Build a Network Analytics report and add 'is curated' and 'curator member ID' as dimensions so curated activity can be separated from open-exchange activity.
Break the report out by inventory so each curated deal's activity can be traced back to the ad units or placements it ran against.
Note any case where premium inventory shows curated activity the publisher did not intend, or where intended inventory shows no curated activity at all.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active curated deal, the inventory it covers and the curator that owns it, alongside the publisher's stated intent for which inventory should be curated and by whom. A setup where each curated deal's inventory and curator match the publisher's intent passes. A setup where premium inventory sits inside general curated deals, where intended inventory is missing from every package, or where unreviewed curators can overlay the inventory is a fail recorded with the deals and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the inventory and curator for each active curated deal and the publisher's intended curation scope above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the curated deals on {{site_name}} do not map cleanly to the inventory the publisher means to expose to curation. Microsoft Curate overlays a third party's data onto inventory and exposes it to buyers as a deal, so a mapping that is too broad can pull premium placements into a general package while a mapping that is too narrow leaves intended inventory outside every package and short of the added demand. {{voice_recommend}} listing the active curated deals against the inventory each covers and the curators enabled in the Partner Center screen, then reconciling that against the publisher's stated intent for which inventory should be curated and by whom. After the reconciliation, adjust the deal mappings and curator permissions so each curated deal carries the inventory the publisher meant to expose.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are left stale as inventory and audiences change
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package is a snapshot of the inventory and audience data it held when it was built. As ad units are retired, new placements launch, and audience segments are refreshed or deprecated, a package left untouched keeps pointing at the old picture. The result is curated deals that reference inventory that no longer exists or miss inventory that now fits, so buyer demand routes to a package that has quietly stopped matching the supply behind it.

educate.base:
A curated package built in Microsoft Curate captures the inventory and the audience data it covered at the moment it was set up. Inventory does not hold still. Ad units get retired, new placements launch, page templates change, and the audience segments a curator attaches get refreshed, renamed, or deprecated over time. A package that is never revisited keeps describing the inventory and audiences as they were on the day it was built, which drifts further from the live supply with each of those changes.

The cost of a stale package is quiet because the deal still transacts. A curated deal that references a retired ad unit simply has less inventory behind it than its buyer expects, so fill comes in below what the deal seemed to promise. A package that never picked up newly launched placements leaves that inventory out of the curated demand path, so it competes only in the open exchange. A deal built on an audience segment that has since been deprecated may target an audience that no longer resolves, so the data overlay that justified the curator's margin is no longer doing its job. None of these throw an error. The deal keeps running against a picture that has gone out of date.

Maintenance is the counterweight. A publisher who reviews curated deals on a cadence, retires deals tied to inventory that no longer exists, and folds new inventory into the packages where it fits keeps the curated supply path matching the live inventory. The IAB Tech Lab's Curated Audiences work, formerly Seller Defined Audiences, frames the audience side of this as a standardized way for publishers and data providers to carry first-party audience data into curated deals, which means the segment taxonomy behind a package is itself something that evolves and needs re-checking.

A few signs point to curated packages that have gone stale:
  • Active curated deals reference ad units or placements that have since been retired
  • Inventory launched after a package was built does not appear in any curated deal it would fit
  • No review cadence exists for curated deals, so the last edit predates recent inventory or audience changes
  • Curated fill or activity has fallen on a deal without a corresponding change in buyer demand, which can point to inventory dropping out from underneath it
You may want to confirm that curated deals are reviewed on a regular cadence and reflect the current inventory and audience segments. One approach is to compare each active curated deal against the live inventory and the current segment taxonomy, then retire or refresh the deals that no longer match.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, section structure and template changes are frequent, so a curated deal built around a section that was later reorganized can end up pointing at placements that moved or disappeared. You may want to re-check curated deals after any significant site or template change so the inventory behind them still exists.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages turn over as the catalog changes, and seasonal pages appear and retire on a schedule. You may want to review curated deals tied to commercial inventory after catalog or seasonal changes so the packages do not keep referencing pages that have been pulled.

investigate.intro: Review each active curated deal against the live inventory and the current audience segment taxonomy, and check when each deal was last edited relative to recent inventory or audience changes.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize UI
steps: Open the curated deals view and list the active curated deals with the inventory each targets and the date each was last edited.
For each deal, check whether the ad units or placements it references still exist in the current inventory, and note any that have been retired.
Check whether inventory launched after a deal was built would fit that deal but is absent from it.
Where a deal carries an audience segment, confirm the segment still resolves and has not been deprecated or renamed in the current taxonomy.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize Network Analytics report
steps: Build a Network Analytics report with 'is curated' and 'curator member ID' as dimensions and trend curated activity per deal over recent months.
Look for deals whose curated fill or activity has fallen without a matching change in buyer demand, which can point to inventory dropping out from under the package.
Note which curated deals show no recent activity at all, which makes them candidates for retirement or refresh.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active curated deal, whether the inventory it references still exists, whether newer fitting inventory is absent, whether its audience segments still resolve, and when it was last reviewed. A setup where curated deals are reviewed on a cadence and reflect the current inventory and segment taxonomy passes. A setup where deals reference retired inventory, miss newer inventory, carry deprecated segments, or have no review cadence is a fail recorded with the deals that have gone stale.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each active curated deal, whether its inventory and audience segments are current and when it was last reviewed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that curated deals on {{site_name}} have been left to drift as inventory and audience segments changed. A curated package captures the inventory and data it held when it was built, so as ad units retire, new placements launch, and segments are refreshed or deprecated, a package that is never revisited keeps pointing at an out-of-date picture and routes buyer demand to supply that no longer matches. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each active curated deal against the live inventory and the current segment taxonomy, retiring deals tied to inventory that no longer exists, and folding newer fitting inventory into the packages where it belongs. After the cleanup, set a review cadence so curated deals are re-checked when inventory or audience data changes rather than left static.
